title: Why Full Fine-Tuning Is Expensive
concept: Updating all parameters of a 70B model requires massive GPU memory to store optimizer states for every weight, making training unaffordable for most teams.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is true about full fine-tuning a massive 70B parameter model?

options.text: It is extremely expensive and requires massive GPU memory.
It can easily be done on a standard smartphone or laptop.
It requires no GPU memory because the model is already pre-trained.

exercises.explain: Large language models like a 70B model have 70 billion parameters, which require immense computational power and memory to adjust.

cards.title: The Hidden Price of AI Training
cards.body: Have you ever wondered why training a massive AI model feels like buying a fleet of supercars? It's not just the model size itself, but the hidden memory cost of updating billions of connections at the exact same time!

cards.title: What is Full Fine-Tuning?
cards.body: Full Fine-Tuning is the process of adjusting every single parameter (weight) in a pre-trained model. For a huge model with 70 billion parameters, this requires updating 70 billion values simultaneously. This process needs massive GPU memory to store the model weights, gradients, and optimizer states.

cards.title: The Whole House Paint Job
cards.body: Imagine painting a giant house. Fine-tuning just the front door (PEFT) requires one small bucket of paint. Full fine-tuning means repainting the entire house, including the roof, basement, and every single brick. You will need a fleet of trucks just to carry the paint and supplies!

cards.title: The 70B Math Problem
cards.body: When training a 70 Billion parameter model like Llama, the model weights themselves take up about 140 GB of memory. However, during full fine-tuning, the optimizer states and gradients require an additional 500+ GB of GPU memory! This requires a cluster of multiple expensive A100 GPUs just to start training.

cards.title: Fitting is Not Training
cards.body: Many think that if a 70B model fits on one or two GPUs for running (inference), it will also fit for training. Myth busted! Training requires up to 4 to 16 times more memory than running the model, because the GPU has to store temporary calculations and optimizer data for every single parameter.

cards.title: Comparing Training Methods
cards.body: Look at the difference in resource requirements between modifying everything versus modifying just a tiny fraction of a massive model.

left.text: Updates all 70B weights. Needs multiple high-end GPUs to store massive optimizer states.

right.text: Updates < 1% of weights. Fits on much cheaper, single-GPU setups.

exercises.prompt: Why does training a model require so much more GPU memory than just running it?

options.text: The dataset becomes 10 times larger during training.
You must store gradients and optimizer states for every single parameter.
The model automatically doubles its parameters during training.
GPUs charge extra fees when you write code to change weights.

exercises.explain: During training, the GPU must keep track of optimizer states, gradients, and activations for every parameter, which requires several times more memory than just holding the model weights.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Full fine-tuning requires updating every single connection weight in the model.
exercises.explain: Full fine-tuning updates every single weight in the neural network, which is why it is so memory-intensive.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of why full training is so expensive.
exercises.sentence: Full fine-tuning is expensive because the GPU must store the weights, gradients, and _____ states for every parameter.
exercises.explain: During training, the GPU must store optimizer states and gradients for every single parameter, which is why memory requirements skyrocket.

exercises.prompt: Two AI systems are learning new tasks. Chatbot A updates all 70 billion of its weights. Chatbot B freezes its 70 billion weights and only updates a tiny set of adapter weights. Which one is using a memory-efficient method?
exercises.explain: Chatbot B is using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), which frozen the main weights and only trains a small fraction of parameters, making it cheap and hardware-friendly. Chatbot A is performing expensive full fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: You are advising a startup with a limited budget on how to train a 70B parameter model. Why should you warn them AGAINST using full fine-tuning?

options.text: PEFT updates all weights, while full fine-tuning updates none.
Full fine-tuning updates every parameter, requiring massive memory for optimizer states.
Full fine-tuning requires less memory because it is faster to compute.
PEFT requires more GPUs because it adds extra layers to the model.

exercises.explain: Full fine-tuning requires tracking and updating every single parameter, which demands enormous memory for optimizer states, gradients, and model weights. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) avoids this by updating only a tiny fraction of the weights.
